(Source - SEC.)

Its Sales & Marketing efficiency rate was a reasonably solid 1.7x in the most recent reporting period, and its core operating leverage ratio was flat, at a weak 0.24x.

Elmet’s IPO Plan

ELMT plans to sell shares of its common stock in an IPO that may exceed $115 million, excluding normal (15%) share allotments to underwriters.

Shareholder sale lock-ups will be for the typical 180-day period after the effective date of the registration statement, as filed by the SEC.

Management plans to use the net proceeds of the IPO for:

The principal purposes of this offering are to obtain additional capital to support our operations, to create a public market for our common stock and to facilitate our future access to the public equity markets. We currently intend that the net proceeds received by us from this offering will be used in part for repayment of certain debts, including [i] the Great Falls Term Loan (as defined below) and [ii] the President Line of Credit (as defined below) and [iii] pre-Reorganization intercompany debt owed to AAI, as described below, with the remainder of the proceeds to be put toward working capital, growth capital (as discussed below), and general corporate purposes.

(Source - SEC.)

The company's online roadshow presentation is currently not available, although by SEC regulation, it may not include any information not included in the latest registration statement, so it’s a bit repetitive in that regard.

Management said that the company is not subject to any legal proceedings that it believes would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or operations.

Underwriters for the IPO include Cantor, Needham & Company, Canaccord Genuity and Roth Capital Partners.

Capital And Liquidity Dynamics

At the end of 2025, Elmet had $2 million in cash and equivalents and $115 million in total liabilities, of which $53.5 million was debt.

For the calendar year 2025, the firm generated free cash flow of only $305,000 on $10.4 million in capex against $10.7 million in operating cash flow, so the company has been a capital-intensive operation, at least in 2025.

Working capital was nearly $50 million with a current ratio of 1.83x, so balance sheet coverage is fine, but a very low absolute cash balance indicates tight liquidity that is tied up in receivables and inventory instead of cash.

So, effectively, all of the operating cash flow has been reinvested into the firm’s asset base, which exposes the company if margins fall.

Also, the firm’s debt is primarily floating-rate, so the company may be sensitive to higher rates if inflation picks up in 2026 due to external shocks (like oil prices).

With around $53 million in debt and minimal cash on hand, the IPO will largely be a deleveraging event, shoring up the balance sheet, but not providing the resources to materially accelerate growth.

Elmet Group Is Growing Moderately In A Positive Environment

ELMT has produced moderate top-line revenue growth and gross profit growth, but net profit has declined, and operating cash flow has dropped as well.

Management has been focusing on increasing capacity, investing in specialized assets and various growth projects, so the numbers don’t look as favorable as they might in the run-up to the IPO.

To that end, the company's growth strategy entails producing more output from its existing installed base, expanding through customer-driven product development and increasing its exposure to defense, energy and research programs.

The firm also has a significant acquisition history, and leadership plans to continue with the addition of tuck-in deals to add market scope, technology and customer access.

Elmet operates in multiple markets, critical materials and high-energy microwave products, all of which indicate a moderate growth trajectory in the coming years, although a recent emphasis by the current U.S. administration on reshoring and increasing production of defense products could increase the demand curve substantially over time.

Elmet’s non-China sourcing ability positions it well for the current environment that favors domestic producers for national security and skill reshoring purposes.

The company presents risks to investors, including its reliance on government contracts, which can make for big contract wins or losses, leading to an unpredictable revenue trajectory.

Also, Elmet is a "smaller reporting company" and "emerging growth company," which allows management to produce less information for shareholders, a negative in the public markets, as many of these kinds of company stocks have disappointed investors.

Given the company's levered balance sheet, the IPO proceeds will be used to pay down debt instead of for future growth and development initiatives, a negative for new shareholders.

While the firm has invested in its ability to scale, it remains labor-constrained for its specialized products.

And, since the company operates two segments, it will be difficult to find a direct public comparable for valuation purposes, increasing the uncertainty of whether the IPO will be reasonably priced or overpriced.

With a significant portion of its revenue derived from U.S. government contracts, Elmet has meaningful customer concentration risk.

Also, $17.3 million of its debt is related-party debt, out of $53.5 million in total debt, so the firm has material related-party transactions in its history.

Overall, Elmet appears to be a moderate-growth, strategically important niche defense vendor, but with weakening financial quality and low core operating leverage despite increased production capacity which holds the prospect of operating profit growth.

Institutional investors may be receptive if management's pricing and valuation assumptions are reasonable and overall stock market volatility is reduced.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be determined.

data.title: CorMedix: Waiting On Phase III ReSPECT For A Further Re-Rating (Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

CorMedix Inc. (CRMD) stock has been on the downward slope since early January. Shares hit a mid-$6 low in late March and have seen some slight upside into the $7 range. In my previous coverage, I initiated my Sell rating due to CMS reimbursement changes impacting DefenCath pricing. However, I do see some upside coming from the Phase III ReSPECT readout sometime in Q2 '26. In the longer run, this could unlock a $2 billion prophylaxis market for Rezzayo and shift the company toward a sort of higher-margin, speciality hospital model. In the near term, however, the trial readout should serve as a meaningful catalyst, which is why I am choosing to upgrade to a Hold rating until we see that readout.

FY25 review

Just as a quick recap on earnings. For Q4 '25, the company reported net revenue of about $128.6 million, which was a big jump year-over-year from the $31.2 million we saw back in Q4 '24. We can put the big driver down to about $91.2 million in DefenCath sales and $37.4 million from Melinta’s portfolio. So I think we can say that there’s been a successful market penetration and adoption among outpatient dialysis customers for DefenCath. The Melinta acquisition, on the other hand, contributed a full quarter of revenue for the first time, which was nice to see. However, they missed on EPS expectations with just $0.16 per diluted share, but they also managed to achieve net income of $14.0 million. It’s worth noting that this was supported by deferred tax assets that offset a bit of the tax expense. Adjusted EBITDA actually surged to $77.2 million compared to $15.3 million last year, so for me, this is a sign of decent underlying profitability and efficient cost management, even if we are seeing increased operational expenses.

As for FY25, CorMedix’s total revenue hit $311.7 million, with pro forma revenue, which would include a full year of Melinta operations, coming to about $401.3 million. So there’s clearly a significant contribution of the acquisition to overall growth here. Elsewhere, net income managed to reach to $163.1 million, or about $2.04 per diluted share, compared with a net loss of $17.9 million back in 2024. Again, this is down to both revenue growth and their use of those deferred tax assets. In terms of spending, operating expenses more than doubled year-over-year to $125.6 million, again from the Melinta acquisition, but also from the integration costs and stock-based compensation we saw throughout the year. They’re also continuing to invest in research and development, particularly for expanded indications of DefenCath.

Coming to liquidity, things are still quite strong. They hold about $148.5 million in cash and short-term investments, which should support ongoing clinical programs and the newly announced $75 million share repurchase initiative. In terms of guidance, CorMedix maintains 2026 guidance of $300 to $320 million in net revenue and $100 to $125 million in adjusted EBITDA. We also have some upcoming clinical milestones, including Phase 3 results for Rezzayo prophylaxis, which could potentially drive some much-needed growth.

Current pipeline catalysts

Now, CorMedix’s clinical pipeline is mainly focused on two key programs, both of which could meaningfully expand its commercial footprint beyond just DefenCath’s current hemodialysis indication. The most advanced candidate would be Rezzayo/rezafungin, which is a once‑weekly echinocandin antifungal that is already approved in the U.S. for treating both candidemia and invasive candidiasis. However, the next step for Rezzayo would be an expansion into prophylaxis of invasive fungal infections for adults undergoing allogeneic blood and marrow transplant.

It’s a rather tight but quite meaningful niche with the current evaluation being done in the global Phase III ReSPECT study. Enrollment for this trial is already complete, with top‑line data expected sometime in Q2 '26. So, this would make it a big near-term catalyst. The aim here would be expanded indication and drive growth in a pretty high‑unmet‑need population.

Other than Rezzayo, CorMedix is also looking to advance DefenCath into a few new clinical settings. So, beyond just its established use to prevent catheter‑related bloodstream infections in adult hemodialysis patients, the company has now initiated a Phase III study in total parenteral nutrition patients. That would then target the prevention of central line‑associated bloodstream infection. We can expect a completion sometime in H1 '27.

Rezzayo expansion

As you know, the Phase III ReSPECT trial is going to be quite pivotal for CorMedix’s share price recovery, so it’s worth taking a quick look at what kind of results we need to see. It’s a global, randomized, double-blind study designed to evaluate Rezzayo as a prophylactic antifungal therapy in high-risk allogeneic blood and marrow transplant (BMT) patients. Now, in simple terms, that means Rezzayo being used as a prophylactic antifungal therapy, so giving the drug before a fungal infection happens, rather than waiting to treat one after it appears. Specifically, it would be for patients undergoing bone marrow transplants since it’s usually the case that they have extremely weak immune systems. It’s high risk since even a small fungal exposure can turn into a serious, life-threatening infection.

So prophylaxis is like a preventive shield against this, with the main goal being to stop infections from developing in the first place. As a brief example, imagine a patient who just received a bone marrow transplant. Their immune system is going to be temporarily wiped out, so they’re now at high risk of infections from fungi that are usually harmless to us. In the current setting, a doctor would typically give daily antifungal pills such as azoles to try to prevent infection. But this is where Rezzayo can offer a bit of differentiation. You see, instead of taking medication every day, the patient could simply receive a once-weekly IV dose that stays in the body at effective levels the entire time. Which would continuously protect them during that high-risk period.

The key difference here from the currently approved use is down to both timing and purpose. Right now, Rezzayo is approved to treat active fungal infections like candidemia. So that means it’s used after a patient is already infected. But in the prophylaxis setting, it would be used much earlier, before infection occurs, to prevent it entirely. It’s a big shift, but also very important because prevention in these patients can reduce hospitalizations significantly. This could be particularly true considering the drug is easier to use and has fewer drug interactions than the current daily therapies.

Mechanistically speaking, rezafungin would be a next-generation echinocandin which can inhibit β-1,3-D-glucan synthesis. Now, that synthesis is a pretty essential component of the fungal cell wall, so by stopping it, it can then lead to cell lysis and death. It’s also worth mentioning that what differentiates rezafungin, and makes it particularly well-suited for prophylaxis in my opinion, is its exceptionally long half-life, which we see at around 130 plus hours. That’s what allows for a once-weekly dosing with decent sustained drug exposure. Going forward, this is a major advantage in transplant patients, who we typically see needing complex, multi-drug daily prophylaxis regimens. These are often limited by toxicity, drug-drug interactions, especially with regard to chemotherapy, and also some adherence challenges. So, in having a single-agent, long-acting antifungal that covers things like Candida and Aspergillus, this overall process could be simplified a lot.

Phase III ReSPECT trial

As for the trial design, ReSPECT is structured as a head-to-head comparison against the standard antimicrobial prophylaxis, as you would expect. The bull case here is that we see Rezzayo outperform the competition, which typically consists of azoles like fluconazole or posaconazole.

Patients are being randomized to receive either once-weekly IV rezafungin at a 400 mg loading dose followed by 200 mg weekly for about 13 weeks or the multi-drug standard regimen. The primary endpoint is set as fungal-free survival at Day 90, which is actually a composite endpoint that is set to capture both the prevention of invasive fungal disease (IFD) and survival. In terms of a clinically/regulatorily meaningful outcome, I do see a lot of upside here if results are positive. And by a positive outcome, I mean the trial must at minimum demonstrate non-inferiority here, with superiority being ideal. We also need to see some favorable secondary outcomes, such as reduced incidence of IFD and lower discontinuation due to toxicity.

As for the all-cause mortality, it should be comparable. Just to mention the FDA perspective here, I think success would require not just statistical non-inferiority in fungal-free survival, but also a nice, clean safety profile and evidence of some clinical advantages. The main advantages I see the FDA focusing on would be fewer adverse events or meaningful reductions in drug-drug interactions. This is especially true when we consider that prophylaxis is administered to vulnerable but not yet infected patients. As for a timeline, we can expect the data readout in Q2 '26, and if results are positive, I’d expect CorMedix to then submit a supplemental NDA to the FDA thereafter. So the commercial opportunity is not so much the short-term catalyst but rather a positive data readout.

A word on the TAM

Just to touch briefly on the TAM for Rezzayo in the expanded prophylaxis setting for allogeneic bone marrow transplant patients, I see it as a pretty clearly defined and commercially meaningful opportunity. The big driver here would be the concentrated but high-value patient population. CorMedix is estimating about 130,000 eligible patients annually in the U.S., which should translate into an implied TAM of over $2 billion for this indication alone. It's worth noting that there is a very high per-patient value here because of the severity of the condition. Now, these would be critically immunocompromised patients where preventing IFDs could significantly reduce mortality and ICU stays. What's important to consider is that prophylaxis is not a one-time dose but a multi-week regimen over about 13 weeks in the ReSPECT trial, which means each patient would then represent a full course of therapy rather than a single treatment event.

As for pricing, it’s still rather early to be considering what price Rezzayo would likely see, but it would be positioned as a premium hospital-administered therapy. That being said, it would still be anchored to existing antifungal standards of care. Current echinocandins and azole-based prophylaxis regimens can cost well into several thousand dollars per treatment course, and given Rezzayo’s differentiated profile of a once-weekly dosing, reduced drug-drug interactions, and its overall broad pathogen coverage, I’d expect it could reasonably command a total course price in the $15,000 to $25,000 range per patient in the U.S.

This would also align with the implied TAM math at about 130,000 patients, giving us a roughly $2 billion market. Now, the driver of pricing power here will not just be efficacy, but rather pharmacoeconomic benefit. In the sense that if Rezzayo can demonstrate fewer hospitalizations and lower toxicity, payers may accept a premium price since there’s the added benefit of a simpler dosing regimen. So I see the market less about volume expansion and more about value capture per high-risk patient here. With a potential set-up being Rezzayo as a high-margin, specialty hospital drug with strong reimbursement support if that Phase III data is compelling.

Valuation

CorMedix is currently trading at a market cap of about $536.8 million with an enterprise value nearing $526.8 million. They also have a pretty balanced capital structure with $148.5 million in cash and $148.9 million in total debt.

So, from a valuation standpoint, the stock would seem to be pretty attractively priced relative to both historical and sector benchmarks. The forward price-to-earnings ratio on a non-GAAP basis sits at 8.43x, which is well below the Health Care sector median of 17.22x. So that tells me the market is heavily discounting their future earnings potential despite the FY25 net income we saw of $163.1 million and pro forma revenue of $401.3 million. Elsewhere, the EV to EBITDA ratio of 4.38x on a forward basis is a lot lower than the sector median of 11.28x, which again would imply that the market is undervaluing the company’s operational profitability and cash flow generation.

I think we can put this discount mainly down to investor caution around execution risks related to their ongoing clinical programs. A good deal of caution would also be priced in around the integration of the Melinta acquisition, and the variability of future revenue growth, especially from DefenCath and Rezzayo. So overall, the market seems to be pricing CorMedix more as a lower-risk, underappreciated growth play, where the current multiples could potentially imply modest revenue expansion, but there are still some conservative assumptions about successful Phase 3 clinical outcomes.

Looking ahead

So just to conclude here, CorMedix is a stronger business than its current valuation suggests. There was solid FY25 profitability and meaningful revenue scale following the Melinta acquisition on last earnings. So this changes the picture somewhat going into Q2 '26. However, the investment case ultimately hinges on execution here, both commercially with DefenCath and clinically with Rezzayo. The upcoming Phase III ReSPECT data in Q2 '26 is the next defining catalyst where I see the upside coming from. There's a potential to unlock a sizable, high-margin prophylaxis market and materially shift future investor sentiment.

So, to restate my thesis, CorMedix is a discounted, catalyst-driven opportunity, in which the market is pricing in execution risk and conservative growth assumptions. If we do see positive Phase III Rezzayo data, this is clear potential for a valuation re-rating. But the downside still remains from the clinical uncertainty and near-term commercial pressures. Which is why I am now choosing to upgrade my CRMD rating to a Hold.

data.title: TSMC: Much More Than Just An AI Play, Recent Jitters Present A Great Opportunity
data.description:
Introduction

Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) recently retreated from its all-time highs, and with the earnings coming up over the next two weeks, I wanted to give an update on what to expect and what I’m looking for, and also give some comments on why I think TSMC is not just a pure-AI play that should be in everyone’s portfolios for the long haul.

Since the last time I covered the company back in October 2025, the company's share price has continued to perform well, gaining over 17% vs SPY's (SPY) 2.5% decline. With the recent decline in share price, I am maintaining my strong buy rating, and will gladly pick up more if the geopolitical landscape due to President Trump's action continues to rock the markets.

What to Expect

Analysts are expecting TSM to make around $3.30 in EPS on $34.88B in revenues. That is around 5% sequential growth and a whopping 38% y/y growth. The upcoming quarter will continue the trend of expanding demand for advanced nodes and will essentially cap off a full year of over $30B in revenue, setting what looks like a new floor for operating performance. In the last 90 days, there have only been four upward revisions to the EPS. And how do these numbers stack up to what management guided for in its last quarter? Management expects sales to be in the range of $34.6B-$35.8B, or $35.3B at the midpoint, with an impressive gross margin range of 63-65%, as well as 54-56% operating margin. On top of these numbers, management raised its full-year 2026 expectations and said that revenue should grow at around 30% in US dollar terms, with capex reaching $52B-$56B.

So, we can see that the analysts are underestimating TSMC’s potential as they believe the company will make less than $35B, while the company itself is guiding for around $35B at the midpoint. How likely is the company to beat analysts’ expectations? If the last ten quarters are any indication, it is very likely, since it has beaten them 100% of the time. Both on top and bottom lines. The beats were also quite decent.

I have a feeling that we may see the company beating its own guidance as well, just because of the insatiable demand for AI products that the company continues to produce hand over fist, with capacity already booked out for at least this year. But is the beat going to be enough to send the company’s shares flying higher? This is a tough one to answer. If we take a look at the previous quarters’ performance on the day of earnings, TSM shares never really made a massive move in either direction. The most recent quarter was the only one in recent quarters that actually had some sort of an upswing of around 7% on the day; the rest were either down 3-4% or up 3-4%. So, if you were planning on playing earnings through buying cheap out-of-the-money calls or puts, or playing iron condors, expect to see your price premiums decline significantly following the report. The expected move on the day is around 7%. Usually, I often see if a move is higher than the expected move; OTM calls tend to win something, especially if that move is over 10%. So, in this instance, buying cheap OTM calls may not be a good idea, but an iron condor might work out. Even if the move ends up being over 10%, your losses will be capped in either direction, allowing you to move on to the next trade.

What I’m looking for

It's no secret that AI is taking over most of the financial operations and reports at many companies. That is especially true for TSM, which is essentially the enabler of all of this AI hype. Without it, we would probably have AI already, too, but not as advanced as what we have right now. NVIDIA (NVDA) may have had the designs of the most advanced chips, but without TSM, there wouldn’t have been anyone to make those designs a reality.

What I’m going to look out for is how the demand has been progressing so far. We know that the capacity has already been booked out for at least 2026. And I wouldn’t be surprised if management is going to say something along the lines of the AI is taking over everything, and because of it, the capacity has been booked out for all of 2027 as well. An executive at Broadcom (AVGO) already hinted at the fact that TSM is nearing capacity limits due to the surging AI demand.

I found an article that says TSMC’s 2nm capacity is already booked beyond 2028 due to the constraints in capacity, but I would take this with a grain of salt until the company itself comes out and either confirms or denies it. The chips are flying off the shelves, so I wouldn’t be surprised to see an order backlog all the way out to 2028. Many memory players in the space are already at capacity and will only be able to provide more of it either at the end of 2027, or sometime in 2028, so it makes sense that the same is happening to TSMC. Capacity takes time to come online.

Recently, it has been confirmed that the company changed some plans regarding the fabs in Japan. TSMC decided to equip its fabs in Japan to handle 3nm production as well, which will be in full operation by 2028. I am expecting to hear more about this news and why the shift in strategy. The most advanced chip production was reserved for its Taiwanese fabs, which carried a lot better unit economics, but it seems like the company may have no other choice but to expand the capacity of the advanced nodes. With this news, I am expecting to get some comments on the overall capex spending for 2026. I am expecting the number they gave us in the last quarter to be upped once again. The previous capex guidance was $52B-$56B, so I am not sure whether this is already including the shift in strategy or not.

TSMC has to follow the steps of the hyperscalers since they are looking to spend close to two-thirds of a trillion dollars in capex this year alone, and that means manufacturing companies like TSM have to expand, or risk losing to competitors. Manufacturing companies are asset-heavy, and the spending that TSMC is already planning to do is one of the biggest capex spending by any manufacturing company. Samsung (SSNLF) is said to plan to spend around $73B in capex for 2026 to advance its AI story, and to take some market share from the king TSMC. I doubt TSM is going to back down to a competitor like Samsung, which is a true competitor in every sense of the word when it comes to advanced nodes like 2nm class process.

Of course, everyone will be looking for any updates to the company’s outlook for the rest of the year because of this demand in AI, which seems to be the only growing sector these days. I don’t think analysts want to see the company merely meeting or beating estimates by a few million in sales and a few cents in EPS. To support the company’s share price going forward, the company needs to come out with a double beat and a raise.

Additionally, and this is probably just my wishful thinking more than anything, I would like to see some sort of inclination to raise prices again because of this ludicrous demand for AI chips. NVDA is enjoying so much more profitability because its GPUs are flying off the shelves and because it doesn’t need to invest a lot in capex due to being a designer and an asset-light model. It’s easy to scale its operations. That is not the same for TSM. Usually, what happens in the past is that the contracts are fixed for a quarter, and then TSM will be able to set new prices accordingly over the following quarters. I would be quite happy to hear that they are planning on raising prices for most of the advanced nodes from 2nm all the way to 5nm and further. I think TSMC has massive leverage here, so it can easily charge for scarce, highly advanced manufacturing capacity, and with the capacity being this tight, customers need to secure slots well in advance, and these customers are more than willing to fork out a pretty penny to continue the AI race.

Beyond AI

I’d like to make a point that even though TSMC may seem like a pure AI play at this point in time, it doesn’t mean that once the hype surrounding AI dissipates, TSMC is going back to obscurity. That is not how this is going to work. TSMC is at the top of the advanced technology enablers, so whatever is the next big thing in tech, you certainly can trust that TSMC will be the one making that next big chip (or small if we take physical size).

It is important to note that the demand from smartphones made up around a third of total sales still. Apple (AAPL) is still one of the biggest customers to date, and it heavily relies on iPhone demand. High-performance computing encompasses not only AI but also 5G, which means not everything is AI GPUs. The company is also very big in automotive and industrial technology.

Additionally, the fabs that are being built right now for AI purposes are essentially just leading-edge fabs and advanced packaging facilities, which means that these facilities will most likely be used for the next major wave of high-end compute, not just AI. I doubt the management is going to build additional AI capacity without thinking about future-proofing these facilities. These are general-purpose advanced manufacturing platforms, which means the next big thing will most likely use the same advanced processes. There may be some upgrades necessary, but nothing needs to be built from scratch.

Valuation

Let’s take a look at what analysts are projecting the company will make, and work off them for my DCF model. Below are the assumptions I used.

Analysts are expecting quite a decent growth in sales and EPS in the short-term, which I agree with, so that is why I went with around 30% growth in sales, and really nice profitability, which I am modeling will come down a bit in the longer run. For the rest of the assumptions, I am using the company’s average historical, or close to historic figures. For example, Capex is going to be much higher than average for now since TSM is spending large amounts to expand, but it will taper off eventually. I am also using the company’s WACC of 8.3% as my discount rate, as I think it is safe enough. And I am also using 2.5% as my terminal growth rate. With these assumptions, we get the following projections.

And with these projections, we get a fair value for TSM to be around $423 a share, which means it is currently trading at a decent discount to its intrinsic value.

Risks and Closing Comments

I think there is a lot of fatigue in playing the AI companies over the past few weeks. Just look at me. I have been actively trying to get away from the names that are essentially pure AI plays, because I fear it is getting quickly out of hand. Look at what has been happening to AI stocks over the last while, now that there has been unrest in Iran. These companies get hit the most because people are taking profits where they can, and that is AI, because AI plays have been the best performers by far.

I saw my AI stocks getting hammered recently, but I haven’t sold because the fear these days has nothing to do with the reality of what’s happening in the AI space fundamentally. The constraints are still in place, the hyperscalers are still going to spend close to a trillion dollars in capital to advance their AI dreams. Just as I am writing this update, Trump is already threatening to hit Iran once again. There is a lot of uncertainty in the markets, and a lot of whipsawing, just like when we saw the Liberation Day nonsense. The only predictable thing about this administration is the fact that Trump is likely to say something to rock the markets again.

I am, for once, welcoming the chaos, as I wouldn’t mind getting more TSMC even cheaper than where it was a few weeks ago. I don’t really call TSMC a pure-AI play because it isn’t. It’s going to be around for decades to come and will continue to fuel every other tech advancement. I view TSMC as a buy-and-forget. No losing sleep at night. I already picked up some shares on this recent downturn, and I will be picking up more if we see a lot more turbulence over the next month or so.

data.title: Micron: The Peak Memory Myth
data.description:
Fears of peak memory are overblown in our opinion, and one victim since reporting its stellar earnings just a few weeks ago has been Micron Technology, Inc. (MU). Make no mistake at all that Micron’s earnings power has simply been incredible, and the growth so rapid, the market is questioning whether it is sustainable.

Despite management’s positive outlook and the consensus views from analysts on the growth, Micron still has a muted multiple relative to many other names in the AI revolution. In fact, the stock rallied tremendously off of a rare public Strong Buy call we made on Micron in late 2025, but has since given all those wonderful gains back.

As members of BAD BEAT Investing know, we take some of those profits when we are up over 25% in a short-time, but do like to let a house position run. But the reset has been painful.

With that said, Micron has been a very popular ticker in our chatrooms, and we have provided a number of thoughts. The day shares dipped back to about $320 we noted the selloff as incredible, and that it was near the top of our list to buy once again.

While we have bounced mildly off of that recent take, we still think these levels are very buyable, and do not buy into the peak memory thesis. In this column we discuss reasons for the selloff and note the strong performance. In our opinion this has been caused by many people taking Alphabet/Google (GOOG) at their word that less memory will be needed due to TurboQuant, and fears that higher capex out of Micron will not bear fruit. Let us discuss.

The thesis here is simple for Micron and that is its position in the broader AI stack. The company sells memory and storage solutions that are used all over the place. Not only in data center cloud servers, but also in many devices for office, personal use, cars and trucks, etc. With AI being utilized more and more the amount of memory needed to run inquiries and models has surged given the complexity of the input requests and the amount of calculation that goes into formulating responses.

This is where the recent news out of Google weighed a bit. In case you missed it, Google recently unveiled a technology called TurboQuant, designed to drastically compress the key-value cache memory necessary for large language model inference. It would seem many are simply taking the company at its word that this will reduce global need for memory, perhaps as competitors adopt similar technology. At the most basic level Google assets this method can reduce the memory footprint by at least 6x without sacrificing model accuracy, which could theoretically cut the demand for memory in AI operations. It did not take long for memory makers like Micron and its competitors to see their stocks fall further following the announcement.

There are a few reasons we do not buy into peak memory, despite this seemingly bearish news out of Google. First, if it can reduce memory demand, would that not lead to pushing the envelope and users employing the highest intensity computing demands it could think of? That is, while reducing current need, it could drive even more intense demand, actually increasing memory demand.

Second, right now, demand and orders still remain high. Demand is not going to dry up overnight. Memory makers are still currently prioritizing high-margin high bandwidth memory for data centers. That will not change for at least a few quarters and by doing so it has caused a hit to the supply of components for consumer electronics like PCs and phones, which has driven prices higher. While there has been some moderation very recently in memory prices, it remains very elevated.

Third, the demand for memory in data centers has also strained manufacturing capacity, meaning there is a large backlog of orders to be filled.

Now, not many are talking about how the agentic AI models are often trial and error. That is our fourth point, a lot of these fail, meaning back to the drawing board to revamp and retool. While the models in a general sense are so much better than just two years ago, this constant failure and refinement keeps demand high. In an effort to be balanced, recognizing the high failure rate, it could lead to companies giving up on those efforts, hurting demand. But as of right now, we cannot see the space being near peak memory demand. As an aside, it reminds us of the peak oil fears of the 1990s and early 2000s.

While that is the broader view, Micron itself saw investor concern over its announcement on capex. To us, it was a bit of a weak excuse to sell the stock, but rapid-returns were made and investors were locking in gains, triggering more selling. The fear again being that if all this money is being spent, and we are near a peak memory event, then the return on investment would be poor. While this is a potentially real and fundamental issue for the company, the market is probably overstating its concern near-term, shooting first for the sell button to ask questions later.

What is the major concern? Well Micron boosted its 2026 capital spending plan by $5 billion, or about 25% to bring its 2026 capital investments to over $25 billion. However, we think this spending announcement was less of this issue. While spending concerns for the aforementioned reasons were evident, what really gave investor’s anxiety in our opinion was that management had also indicated that its capital spending would rise again in 2027. So this spending caused fear of too much supply. However, as we assert, there is simply no sign that too much supply is on the horizon.

With that said what investors may be missing is that Micron and related names dealing with memory have always been cyclical. But they broke that cyclicality with unprecedented demand, almost becoming secular. That is, enjoying a constant stream of demand. The recent trading suggests the market is treating Micron and the competition like it will be going back to being cyclical. That all remains to be seen, but the data right now strongly suggests that memory supply needs a lot more time to truly catch up to demand.

And so, with an incredible reset in shares, with expectations for this fiscal year to see nearly 600% EPS growth from last fiscal year, and fiscal 2027 to see 70% EPS growth, shares are absurdly cheap right now. If EPS expansion dries up in fiscal 2028, which is many quarters away, it would still be cheap. But Fiscal 2028 does not start for over 18 months. Typically, the market would not front load a lack of growth this soon into share prices, especially with two more quarters remaining in fiscal 2026, and a stock now trading at 6.3X the consensus.

We believe MU stock has overshot to the downside in this correction, especially the closer it gets to $300. Shares are a buy in our opinion once again.

data.title: Risk Off After Trump's Speech - Intraday Outlook On S&P 500, Nasdaq 100, AUD/USD, Gold, And WTI Crude Oil
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

US President Trump’s evening prime-time speech on Wednesday, 1 April at 9.00 p.m. Washington time does not offer any new information to global financial markets. A quick recap: Trump highlighted earlier on Tuesday, March 31, that the US-Iran war will “end soon” within the next two to three weeks.

Interestingly, Trump has indirectly issued a more “hawkish rhetoric” towards the Iranian leadership in his prime-time national address, stating that the US will hit Iran “extremely hard” in the next two to three weeks, and added that the US will also strike Iran’s electric plants if there is no deal.

Overall, there is no clear indication of a forthcoming “de-escalation” from Trump, which in turn brings the stagflation risk narrative back to the forefront again.

How do the markets react? Risk-off mode at this time of writing.

  • WTI crude oil +5.8% at around $104 per barrel
  • Brent crude oil +6.4% at around $106 per barrel
  • US Dollar Index +0.4%
  • AUD/USD -0.6%
  • Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) -1.7%
  • S&P 500 and Nasdaq 100 E-mini futures -1.1% and -1.3%
  • Japan’s Nikkei 225 -2.2%
  • Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Index -1.1%
  • China’s CSI 300 -0.7%
  • South Korea’s KOSPI 200 -4.7%
  • Singapore’s Straits Times Index -0.7%

We have highlighted in our earlier detailed report that the prior three-day rebound seen on the three major benchmark US stock indices, S&P 500, Nasdaq 100, and Dow Jones Industrial Average are likely a mean reversion rebound sequence (dead cat bounces) within their respective medium-term downtrend phases.

Let’s focus now on the short-term trajectory (1-3 days) on the S&P 500, Nasdaq 100, AUD/USD, gold (XAU/USD), and WTI crude oil from a technical analysis perspective.

S&P 500 - Bearish reaction at descending channel resistance with bearish momentum

The price actions of the US S&P 500 CFD index (a proxy of the S&P 500 E-mini futures) have staged a bearish reaction right after a retest on its 20-day moving average and upper boundary of a descending channel in place since February 26, 2026 high (see Fig. 1).

In addition, the hourly RSI momentum indicator has staged a bearish breakdown below its former ascending trendline support after it flashed a bearish divergence condition at its overbought zone.

Watch the 6,600 key short-term pivotal resistance, and a break below 6,455 near-term support is likely to trigger another potential bearish impulsive down-move sequence to expose the next intermediate supports at 6,327/6,290 and 6,218/6,210.

However, a clearance above 6,600 invalidates the near-term bearish scenario for an extension of the mean reversion rebound towards the 6,648/6,730 zone.

Nasdaq 100 - Bearish reaction below 20-day moving average with bearish momentum

The price actions of the US Nasdaq 100 CFD index (a proxy of the Nasdaq 100 E-mini futures) have staged a bearish reaction below its downward-sloping 20-day moving average, coupled with the hourly RSI momentum indicator’s bearish breakdown below its key ascending trendline support (see Fig. 2).

Watch the 24,047/24,177 key short-term pivotal resistance, and a break below 23,400 near-term support is likely to trigger another potential bearish impulsive down move sequence to expose the next intermediate supports at 23,085 and 22,800/22,680.

On the other hand, a clearance above 24,177 invalidates the near-term bearish scenario for an extension of the mean reversion rebound towards the 24,355/24,537 zone.

AUD/USD – En route towards the lower limit of the descending channel

The minor corrective mean reversion rebound seen on the AUD/USD from its swing low of 0.6838 printed on Monday, March 30, 2026, is likely to be over (see Fig. 3).

Watch the 0.6910/0.6938 key short-term pivotal resistance, and a break below 0.6838 is likely to trigger a potential fresh bearish impulsive down move sequence to expose the next intermediate supports at 0.6780/0.6760 and 0.6710 (also close to the key 200-day moving average).

However, a clearance above 0.6938 invalidates the near-term bearish scenario for an extension of the mean reversion rebound towards the next intermediate resistance at 0.7000/0.7015 (also the 50-day moving average).

Gold (XAU/USD) - At risk of a “bearish flag” breakdown below $4,775 key resistance

The recent seven-day rally (high to low) of 17% from the March 23, 2026 low seen in gold is likely to be a corrective mean reversion rebound in the form of a “bearish flag” within its medium-term downtrend phase (see Fig. 4).

The bullish momentum of the 7-day rally is getting exhausted. The hourly RSI momentum indicator has just staged a bearish breakdown below its key ascending trendline support after a prior bearish divergence condition at its overbought zone

Watch the $4,775 key medium-term pivotal resistance, and a break below $4,524/4,486 support (the lower boundary of the “bearish flag”) is likely to trigger another potential minor bearish impulsive down move sequence in the first step to expose the next intermediate supports at $4,319 and $4,167/4,099 (also the key 200-day moving average).

On the flip side, a clearance above $4,775 invalidates the near-term bearish scenario for an extension of the mean reversion rebound towards the next intermediate resistance at $4,980/5,039 (also the 50-day moving average).

WTI crude oil - Bulls find support above 20-day moving average, traded back above $102.25

The intraday sell-off seen on Wednesday, April 1, 2026, for West Texas oil CFD (a proxy of the WTI crude oil futures) has been erased as its price actions trade back above $102.25 (see Fig. 5).

Watch the $96.44/93.70 key short-term pivotal support (close to the 20-day moving average) to maintain a potential fresh minor impulsive up move sequence towards $116.56/119.54 in the first step within its medium-term uptrend phase.

A clearance above $116.56/119.54 opens scope for the next intermediate resistance to come in at $124.40, followed by the major resistance of $131.30/132.67 seen during the onset of the Russia-Ukraine war.

On the other hand, a break below $93.70 negates the bullish tone for a slide towards the medium-term pivotal support zone of $88.36/86.50.

Original Post
